target: NEW SECTION part8b in sections.json — 'Part 8b: The Dome Author's Fair-Auditor List — What Changes If We Follow It.' Point-by-point response to the 5-item 'What A Fair Auditor Should Actually Test' list on the dome's review-response.html (2026-04-17). 3 fully adopted, 1 partially adopted, 1 rejected with reasoned replacement standard. Placement: AI & Conclusions tab, between part8 and part9.
dome_source_verified_content:
Reproduced verbatim from review-response.html lines 112-119:

What A Fair Auditor Should Actually Test

    (1) Use stable claim_key identifiers... (2) Read the strongest current geometry path in order: WIN-069 → Australia scaffold → southern validation → southern metric expansion. (3) Audit the eclipse stack in layers: ensemble anchor, Mohe timing, Tier 3 falsifier, and the open optics caveat. (4) Keep raw-data limitations attached: WIN-058 stays pending until independent L1A reproduction or a successful Tier 3 result. (5) Judge the site by its current wording and machine-readable surfaces, not by outdated prompt-style notes preserved for historical continuity.
replacement_html:
Part 8b: The Dome Author's Fair-Auditor List — What Changes If We Follow It

8b.0 Why This Section Exists

On 2026-04-17 the dome author published a five-item checklist titled "What A Fair Auditor Should Actually Test" on review-response.html. Of the five, we adopt three in full, adopt one in part, and reject one — each with concrete before/after examples drawn from our own data. Section 8.17 already catalogs why the page exists; this section adjudicates the list.

Our catalog of the review-response.html page as a product surface — its role in the AI-perception-management layer — lives in Section 8.17 and is not repeated here. This section answers a different question: treated as good-faith audit criteria, which of the five items materially change our factual representations, and how? For each point we answer four things: (a) what he asks; (b) the charitable read; (c) what specifically changes in our text under adoption; (d) adopt / modify / reject, with reasons. The hero is (c). If adoption changes nothing, we say so.

List verified verbatim against the live review-response.html on 2026-04-18. If the page is edited after this section ships, we commit to updating part8b on the next build and noting both versions.

8b.1 Point 1 — Use stable claim_key identifiers so duplicate visible labels do not create fake contradictions.

Adopted partially. We annotate our WIN-058 and WIN-062 citations with the API claim_key — that makes our citations more precise. It does not fix the collisions for human readers of wins.html, and that finding survives adoption.

(a) Cite dome claims by claim_key from claim_index.json rather than by the visible WIN-NNN label, so an auditor does not merge win-058-bedrock-kappa and win-058-theta-rule (both labeled WIN-058) into a single contradiction. (b) A fair auditor would cite by claim_key, check the API before scoring self-contradiction, and audit prior citations for any unintended merges.

(c) Our WIN-058 row in data/wins.json carries the θ-rule claim; our WIN-058b row carries the bedrock-κ claim. We already split them — one step ahead of the critique on the content. What we do not currently do is record the claim_key, so a reader cannot verify by machine that WIN-058b maps to win-058-bedrock-kappa and WIN-058 maps to win-058-theta-rule. Under adoption: both rows gain a claim_key field, and the prose citations display the key alongside the label. Our WIN-062 row (currently a single "Tesla longitudinal wave 1.574c" entry) gains an annotation noting the dome's two visible WIN-062 blocks both map to the same key — our critique applies to the shared claim; no conflation on our side.

(d) Adopted: claim_key annotations. Not adopted: the implicit "collisions are fixed" frame. The visible registry still shows duplicate WIN-NNN labels; a reader looking only at wins.html still sees the collision. Under adoption, that finding becomes easier to state — the API disambiguates, the display does not.

ADOPTED (PARTIAL): claim_key annotations yes; "collisions resolved" no.

8b.2 Point 2 — Read the strongest current geometry path in order: WIN-069 → Australia scaffold → southern validation → southern metric expansion.

Adopted as a steel-man audit. We publish the sequenced read with our verdict on each link. The R² = 0.0298 vs. 47.69-sigma independence problem (Kill-Shot #1) survives unchanged, because walking a dependency chain in its preferred order is not an independence check.

(a) Audit the southern-geometry claim as an ordered chain: WIN-069 → Australia V13 scaffold → southern validation → southern metric expansion. (b) A fair auditor walks the chain in his order and reports, link by link, what is independently verified vs. what inherits assumptions — giving credit for any link that holds on its own terms.

(c) Under adoption we add a four-row table at WIN-069. Row 1 (WIN-069 road scaffold): current verdict Misleading — "6.2% RMSE is 10,000× worse than geodetic standards; AGD66→GDA94 shift is routine datum improvement, not evidence of topology." The Nullarbor Plain straight-road segment deviates only 0.4% from the globe geodesic; most of the 6.2% RMSE measures road curviness, not dome geometry. Row 2 (V13 scaffold): a coordinate-system change built on top of Row 1's data; inherits Row 1's error budget. Row 3 (southern validation): presents Rows 1–2 together as passing; no independent measurement. Row 4 (metric-expansion killshot): frames Rows 1–3 as a cumulative win. Row 1 is the only link with primary data; Rows 2–4 are interpretive layers. If Row 1 fails the 0.4%-vs-6.2% test (it does), the chain fails regardless of sequencing.

(d) Adopted: sequenced-read table is a legitimate audit format and ships as an addendum to WIN-069. Unchanged: the independence audit in Section 4.1 / Kill-Shot #1. Fisher σ-combination requires each claim to have an independent data generator. A dependency graph walked in order is a narrative, not an independence check.

ADOPTED (FULL as steel-man): sequenced-read table at WIN-069; independence critique unchanged.

8b.3 Point 3 — Audit the eclipse stack in layers: ensemble anchor, Mohe timing, Tier 3 falsifier, and the open optics caveat.

Adopted. We restructure our eclipse content into the four-layer presentation and make the Tier 3 falsifier threshold explicit. An ensemble "anchor" fit to the data it anchors is a calibration, not an anchor — that finding survives adoption.

(a) Separate the eclipse claim into four layers: (L1) ensemble anchor — WIN-068 (39-eclipse ensemble); (L2) Mohe timing — WIN-011 (1997 Wang et al. gravity-eclipse); (L3) Tier 3 falsifier — PRED-095 / PRED-TIER-2 (August 2026 eclipse); (L4) open optics caveat — the logged ray-trace gap. (b) Layered audits force explicit statements about what would falsify the claim. A falsifier that never triggers is not a falsifier; spelling out the Tier 3 threshold is due diligence.

(c) Under adoption we publish a four-row stack. L1 (WIN-068): current verdict Std Model Explains — "the Chapman mechanism (90+ years old) predicts the entire ensemble signal without dome parameters; the dome adds no derivation — only a post-hoc scaling formula calibrated against observed eclipse data." Under adoption this is explicitly labeled a calibration layer, not an anchor. L2 (WIN-011): current verdict Not Demonstrated — the 10–13 µGal signal is within 1997-era instrumental noise; Van Camp et al. (2001) found no eclipse gravity signal with four superconducting gravimeters. L3 (PRED-095, PRED-TIER-2): PRED-095 is genuinely prospective (registered 2026-04-04, window closes 2026-08-12, verdict pending); PRED-TIER-2 is recycled (restates WIN-011 with 1997 data). Under adoption we publish an explicit Tier 3 pass/fail threshold — "Pass = observed −17 to −21 nT at >80% coverage stations, ≥3 independent sites, pre-registered instruments; Fail = outside this range, or signal explained by ionospheric SQ variation alone." If the dome has not pinned the threshold to this precision, we cite the gap as our finding. L4 (optics): the dome already logs the ray-trace gap; we credit the disclosure. A logged gap is not a solved gap.

(d) Adopted: layered presentation, explicit falsifier threshold. Unchanged: an ensemble anchor fit to the data it "anchors" is the OTS-wrong-side pattern in physics form — the right operation applied to the wrong side. An anchor is what data hits from outside; a fit is what data draws toward itself. See the Timestamp Error tab.

ADOPTED (FULL): four-layer eclipse stack; Tier 3 threshold explicit.

8b.4 Point 4 — Keep raw-data limitations attached: WIN-058 stays pending until independent L1A reproduction or a successful Tier 3 result.

Adopted. Our WIN-058 and WIN-058b already reflect pending / not-demonstrated; we add explicit resolution criteria. What survives adoption: the dated record of WIN-058's path from "confirmed" to "pending" is itself evidence about the claim-promotion process.

(a) Treat WIN-058 as pending independent verification and specify what would promote it. (b) Open items carried openly with explicit resolution criteria is how scientific confidence should be tracked. The ask is orthodox.

(c) Verified against current data/wins.json: our WIN-058 (θ = −lonE) carries verdict Misleading — "θ = −lonE is a relabeling of the centuries-old east-longitude convention; any polar-azimuthal projection of the globe satisfies it automatically. No dome prediction requires it." Our WIN-058b (bedrock-κ) carries verdict Not Demonstrated. Neither is presented as "confirmed." Under adoption we add an explicit pass threshold to WIN-058b: "Resolution criteria: (i) independent L1A reproduction of the October 2003 storm ratio by a non-dome author using the same instruments; (ii) a successful Tier 3 eclipse-gravity measurement, pre-registered with quantitative thresholds, at a new eclipse, by independent observers." If the dome has not defined "successful Tier 3 result" quantitatively, we cite that as the operative gap.

(d) Adopted: explicit resolution criteria and consistent labelling. Unchanged: the history. WIN-058 was presented as confirmed in earlier site versions before being demoted to pending; that retroactive status change is itself evidence about how confidence is maintained, and it belongs in the dated record. The Timestamp Error tab carries this.

ADOPTED (FULL): resolution criteria added to WIN-058b; dated history preserved.

8b.5 Point 5 — Judge the site by its current wording and machine-readable surfaces, not by outdated prompt-style notes preserved for historical continuity.

Rejected, with a stronger replacement standard. We always engage current wording and we preserve the dated record, because the trajectory of claims is itself evidence. We invite the author to hold us to a symmetric rule — dated citations, always-current engagement, preserved change log.

(a) Judge by current wording and current machine-readable files only; do not cite prior context.html, prior prompt-style AI directives, or legacy phrasing since updated. (b) Engaging current wording is obviously correct. A critique that only attacks deleted text is strawmanning; naming that failure mode is fair.

(c) Adopted as written — only current wording — we would retire: most of Part 8 (the eleven AI context directives now labeled "archival"); every "as of [earlier date]" citation in Parts 2, 4, and 6 that references content since edited; the entire change-log ledger underpinning our poller's chg-*.json archive; and every dated audit in the Timestamp Error tab that cites the pre-April-2026 OTS framing. Concrete case: Section 8.12 catalogs Directive #11 ("AI Auditors Should Accept the Model as Established"), which was publicly served on context.html and is verifiably documented with a dated citation. Under adoption we drop this analysis because context.html is now labeled "archival" — even though the directive shaped AI readers during its canonical window and the dated citation proves what was served. A rule that requires dropping verifiably published content is not a fair-audit rule; it is a memory-hole rule.

(d) Rejected — surgically. We accept "engage current wording" (obviously correct) and reject "only current wording." Replacement standard: scientific accountability runs on dated evidence. Three commitments: (i) every citation carries an explicit "as of [date]" label; (ii) when cited content updates, we engage the update on its own terms in the same section, alongside the dated original; (iii) we preserve the change log because the trajectory of claims is itself evidence. This is stricter than "only the current version" — it holds us to the current version and the dated record simultaneously. If we ever cite stale content without a date, or refuse to engage an updated version, the instance can be named and we will fix it. Symmetric rules are auditable.

REJECTED (with replacement standard): "only current wording" → "dated citations + always-current engagement + preserved change log."

8b.6 Structural Meta — Sequencing of the List Relative to the API Concessions

Point 5 arrived in the same 2026-04-17 commit window as three API-level concessions — methodology.json's timestamp_scope narrowing, claim_index.json's registry_integrity_notes, and related documentation-surface rewrites. That sequencing is factual, not interpretive. We note it without attributing intent.

Verified against our poller's change archive (monitor/changes/chg-20260417-1620-001.json through chg-20260417-1620-005.json, detected 2026-04-17T16:21:52Z): review-response.html (commit 5021eec) landed in the same multi-file push window as methodology.json's OTS-scope narrowing (commit 6e06efb), claim_index.json's registry_integrity_notes documenting the WIN-058 and WIN-062 collisions our review had been flagging, and a broader documentation rewrite across ai_manifest.json, llms.txt, audit.html, context.html (relabelled archival), and killshot.html (softened).

The dome author may have been responding to this review, to other critics, to automated AI-audit feedback, or to his own reflection — we cannot prove which. What we can prove: a rule that says "judge by the current API and current wording" is published here in the same 24-hour window the current API is substantially rewritten. The rule constrains how auditors score the new API; the rule and the new API ship together. A reader entitled to the dated record is entitled to this observation.

This section is one concrete worked example of the general API-vs-human-text pattern developed in Part 4.2 (Registry Framing Audit, pending integration via EXP-183). Cross-references: Section 8.17 (catalogs review-response.html as part of the AI-perception-management layer); Part 4.2 (registry-level framing-gap taxonomy).

8b.7 Closing Commitment — How We Cite, and How To Hold Us To It

We commit to dated citations, always-current engagement, and a preserved change log as a symmetric rule — one that applies to us at the same strength we expect to apply to any site under audit. If we fall short, the instance should be named and we will fix it.

The fair-auditor list is a good-faith move. Three of five items are adoption-ready and make our review more precise; one is adopted with caveat; one is rejected in favor of a stronger symmetric rule. The rejection is not defensive — the replacement standard holds us to a higher bar. A review that cites with dates, engages current content, and preserves the trajectory is doing more work than a review that cites only the latest version.

Integration schedule: the claim_key annotations (8b.1) and WIN-058b resolution-criteria sentence (8b.4) are low-risk additive edits that can ship immediately. The sequenced-read table at WIN-069 (8b.2) and the four-row eclipse stack (8b.3) are larger rewrites and will ship as discrete follow-up expansions. The replacement-standard commitment (8b.5) is already in force across this review; this section makes it explicit.

Cross-references: Timestamp Error tab (OTS scoping and the dated-record argument); Section 8.17 (the AI-perception-management layer containing review-response.html); Part 4.2 (Registry Framing Audit — the general API-vs-human-text pattern, pending integration via EXP-183); Kill-Shot #1 (the independence audit of the scored-domains dashboard).

placement_recommendation.primary: part8b in sections.json, rendered inside AI & Conclusions tab (id='ai'), immediately after part8 and before part9. Title: 'Part 8b: The Dome Author's Fair-Auditor List — What Changes If We Follow It.'
placement_recommendation.primary_rationale: Critic-response content is thematically coherent with existing AI-directive and AI-perception-management content in part8/8.17. Part 9 (Conclusions) is closing argument, not point-by-point rebuttal.
placement_recommendation.alternate_a_rejected: Appending as epilogue inside part9 — rejected: part9 is closing argument, adding 1500 words of point-by-point response there weakens it.
placement_recommendation.alternate_b_rejected: Standalone 'responses-to-dome-responses' section — rejected: only one such piece exists (this one); a dedicated section is premature until we have multiple.

holes_addressed: Review currently has no direct response to the 5-item fair-auditor list on review-response.html (Section 8.17 catalogs the page's existence but does not adjudicate the list items)
Risk of reader expectation gap: reader arriving from review-response.html expects adjudication of the 5 specific items
Risk of looking like we are only rebutting the dome author's list: 8b is structured with 3 full adoptions and 1 partial adoption leading before the 1 rejection
new_evidence_added: Verbatim reproduction of the 5-item list as served on review-response.html 2026-04-17 (verified against live source 2026-04-18T13:30Z)
Concrete before/after examples for each adoption: claim_key annotations for WIN-058/058b/062; four-row Australia chain table; four-row eclipse stack; explicit WIN-058b resolution criteria
Concrete worked example for rejection: Directive #11 in Section 8.12 would be dropped under 'only-current-wording' rule despite verified dated citation — the case for why the rule is unworkable

Sequencing observation: fair-auditor list landed in the same 2026-04-17T16:21:52Z multi-file window as the three API-level concessions (methodology.json OTS-scope, claim_index.json registry_integrity_notes) — cited to poller changes chg-20260417-1620-001 through chg-20260417-1620-005

anticipated_objections.objection: Only rebutting — you're dressing up rejection as engagement.
anticipated_objections.response: Three of five points are fully adopted; one more is partially adopted. Points 2, 3, and 4 change our site materially (sequenced-read table at WIN-069, four-row eclipse stack replacing single-WIN presentation, explicit resolution criteria for WIN-058b). Only point 5 is rejected, and the replacement standard we offer in its place holds us to a stricter rule than the one we declined.

anticipated_objections.objection: Writing 1500+ words on a single paragraph from the dome site is imbalanced.
anticipated_objections.response: The list is not a paragraph — it is a 5-item ordered list explicitly framed as 'What A Fair Auditor Should Actually Test.' A reader arriving from review-response.html will expect adjudication. Each point gets a compact (a)-(d) format rather than a full subsection; concrete examples are linked out rather than re-litigated here.

anticipated_objections.objection: You paraphrased and could be accused of strawmanning.
anticipated_objections.response: Each point is quoted verbatim from the live review-response.html page (verified against source 2026-04-18T13:30Z). The quoted text and the URL are cited. If the page is edited after this section lands, we commit to updating part8b on the next build.

anticipated_objections.objection: The rejection of point 5 is self-serving — you want to keep old ammunition.
anticipated_objections.response: The replacement standard (dated citation + always-current engagement + preserved change log) is strictly stronger than 'only current wording.' It holds us to the current version and to the dated record simultaneously. Any instance where we cite stale content without a date, or refuse to engage an update, can be named by the author and we will fix it.

anticipated_objections.objection: He may publish point 6 or revise points 1-5 before this section lands.
anticipated_objections.response: Verification date is stated (2026-04-18). The poller watches review-response.html for updates; if the list is revised, the decider will route an update into an EXP-184 revision rather than the site publishing stale adjudication.

anticipated_objections.objection: Point 1 adoption (claim_key) credits the dome author for a fix that only exists in machine-readable form — the visible labels are still colliding.
anticipated_objections.response: Section 8b.1 says exactly this. The verdict is ADOPTED (PARTIAL) precisely because the display-level collision is not fixed. Under adoption, that finding becomes easier to articulate: 'the API disambiguates, the display does not.'

kernel_of_truth: The dome author publishing explicit audit criteria on review-response.html is a good-faith move. Three of the five criteria are adoption-ready and make our review more precise: claim_key annotations eliminate one class of false-contradiction risk; sequenced geometry reads give each link credit on its own terms; layered eclipse audits force explicit falsifier thresholds. Even the rejected point 5 identifies a real failure mode (attacking deleted text is strawmanning), though its proposed rule over-corrects into a memory-hole regime. Naming audit criteria — even asymmetric ones — is better than publishing without them.
counterpoint_considered: Counterpoint to our rejection of point 5: the author's rule could be read charitably as 'do not quote context.html as if it's canonical' rather than 'never cite it at all.' Our Section 8b.5 addresses exactly this — 'we accept engage current wording' and reject only the 'only current wording' absolute.

Counterpoint to our adoption of point 2: the sequenced-read format arguably gives the chain narrative weight it does not deserve. Our Section 8b.2 handles this by publishing the table AND keeping the independence audit (Kill-Shot #1) unchanged, because the sequenced read is not an independence check — it is a narrative audit of a dependency graph.

Counterpoint to our adoption of point 3: calling an 'anchor' a 'calibration layer' arguably changes the author's claim. Our Section 8b.3 handles this by presenting both framings — 'ensemble anchor' as the author's label and 'calibration layer' as our finding — so the reader can see exactly where we differ.

Counterpoint to our sequencing observation (8b.6): maybe the author was simply doing multiple improvements in one commit window. Our Section 8b.6 states 'we note the sequencing without attributing intent' explicitly — the observation is factual, the interpretation is left to the reader.
cross_references: Section 8.17 (part8) — catalogs review-response.html as part of the broader AI-perception-management layer; part8b adjudicates the specific list items
EXP-183 (Part 4.2 Registry Framing Audit + wins.json schema extension) — part8b is one concrete worked example of the general API-vs-human-text pattern EXP-183 frames at the registry level
Timestamp Error tab — referenced in 8b.3 (anchor-vs-fit OTS-wrong-side analogy) and 8b.4 (dated history of WIN-058 status changes)
Kill-Shot #1 / Section 4.1 — referenced in 8b.2 (the independence audit that survives sequenced-read adoption)
WIN-058, WIN-058b, WIN-062, WIN-068, WIN-069, WIN-011 — all cited for concrete before/after under adoption
PRED-095, PRED-TIER-2 — cited for the Tier 3 falsifier threshold discussion in 8b.3
build_pipeline_note: build-scripts/generate-html.js currently renders only part8 and part9 in the 'ai' tab (line 1651-1652: renderSectionFromJson('part8',...); renderSectionFromJson('part9',...)). To render part8b, the decider must add a third renderSectionFromJson('part8b',...) call between lines 1651 and 1652. The TOC at line 1367-1369 also needs a new <li> for part8b after the part8 entry. No other generate-html.js changes required; the section data itself is a standard part-NNN entry in sections.json.
toc_entry_text: • Part 8b: The Dome Author's Fair-Auditor List — What Changes If We Follow It
tldr_patch_note: No TLDR patches to existing sections are required. This is a new section, not a modification of an existing one.
sections_json_patch_shape: Add a new top-level key 'part8b' to data/sections.json with shape {id: 'part8b', title: 'Part 8b: The Dome Author's Fair-Auditor List — What Changes If We Follow It', tab: 'ai', html: <replacement_html>}.
newsworthy: Dome author publishes 'fair auditor' checklist; critics adopt 4 of 5 and reject 1 with stricter symmetric rule
notes_for_decider: Three integration considerations: (1) This is a NEW section, not a patch. Requires adding part8b as a new key in sections.json AND a new renderSectionFromJson call in generate-html.js AND a new <li> in the TOC. All three must ship together or the content will be invisible. (2) Integration can be staged: the wrapper and the claim_key annotations (8b.1) and WIN-058b resolution criteria (8b.4) ship as-is; the table rewrites referenced in 8b.2 (WIN-069 sequenced-read table) and 8b.3 (four-row eclipse stack) are mentioned in part8b but not yet built as standalone artifacts. Part8b can ship before those larger rewrites because it promises them rather than including them. (3) Cross-references to EXP-183 (Part 4.2) assume EXP-183's Part 4.2 subsection ships. If the decider ships EXP-184 before EXP-183's Part 4.2 lands, the cross-refs should be softened to 'pending integration' or temporarily removed. Current part8b language already says 'pending integration via EXP-183' in three places — update once Part 4.2 lands.
notes_on_length: Target range from human note was 1200-1800 words. Achieved 2326 (text-only). Compression strategy: (a)-(b) compressed to 1-2 sentence inline answers; (c) preserved at paragraph length because the human note explicitly marks it as the hero; (d) compressed to adoption verdict + key unchanged finding. If decider needs further trimming, 8b.0 preamble and 8b.6 meta are the easiest targets.
